cataloging_subject.label: The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English prose chronicle of James I of Scotland's murder in 1437 and the punishment of the conspirators, translated from a now-lost Latin narrative by John Shirley. The repository encoding follows a defective Add MS 5467-derived print tradition and includes an 1818 title page and anonymous Advertisement; those two print paratexts are retained as zero-count content parts but are not collapsed into the medieval work. The record excludes the lost Latin narrative as a separate work, the three known manuscript witnesses as physical objects, Pinkerton's 1797 transcription, the anonymous 1818 reset, the U-M encoding, and the checked-in XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous; translated from a lost Latin narrative by John Shirley
catalog_summary.editor: John Pinkerton (1797 transcription); anonymous Glasgow reset (1818)
catalog_summary.date: After 1437 and before John Shirley's death in 1456; traditionally circa 1440
catalog_summary.region: English translation concerning Scotland; exact place of composition and translation unknown

identifiers.value: Add MS 5467, ff. 72v-84v and fragment f. 72*

identifiers.scheme: National Library of Scotland shelfmark and locus

titles.value: The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis

assertion.notes: Matheson's modern edition and the encoded body heading use the singular Kynge form; Connolly's article title supplies the variant plural Kings.

titles.value: A Full Lamentable Cronycle of the Dethe and False Murdure of James Stewarde, Last Kynge of Scotys
titles.supplied_by: Add MS 5467 rubric as described by the British Library

titles.value: The dethe of James Kynge of Scotis
notes: The electronic discovery title names Shirley as though he were the author; this manifest retains his narrower translator role.

agents.name: John Shirley
notes: The Add MS 5467 colophon names Shirley as the translator from Latin in his last age. It does not make him the Latin narrative's author or establish that he copied any of the three surviving English witnesses.

scope.description: Middle English translation of the lost Latin narrative, not authorship of that source

assertion.notes: The explicit translator colophon survives in Add MS 5467 and its Pinkerton-derived print descendants; Add MS 38690 and Adv.MS.17.1.22 lack that colophon.

date_statements.display: After James I's murder in 1437 and before John Shirley's death in 1456; traditionally dated circa 1440

assertion.notes: The bounds follow the narrated 1437 murder and the named translator's death. Circa 1440 is the current BL date for the complete Add MS 38690 text and a longstanding scholarly date, not a demonstrated exact translation year.

date_statements.display: The murder of James I on 20-21 February 1437 and its aftermath
Circa 1440 in British Library Add MS 38690

assertion.notes: This is the current catalog date of the complete surviving witness text, not proof that the witness is Shirley's autograph or that translation and copying were simultaneous.

date_statements.display: First known publication by John Pinkerton in 1797

scope.description: The Middle English translation and its English transmission milieu, not the lost Latin narrative's proven origin

assertion.notes: Add MS 5467 was produced in England, Shirley is associated with London, and the 1818 Advertisement argues from internal evidence for an English writer. No inspected source fixes the translation place or the Latin source's place of composition.

scope.description: Blackfriars at Perth as the principal murder setting

assertion.notes: The XML header declares only Latin, but direct inspection shows a Middle English work; Latin names the lost source language and is not an intrinsic language of the surviving translated text.

scope.description: The brief Jean de Meun maxim on the 1818 title page and in the chronicle's closing counsel

assertion.notes: This is a short embedded quotation, not a sustained second-language section.

form_statements.description: A continuous prose chronicle with a brief four-line verse close. The encoded BODY contains 31 P elements and one LG of four L elements; the closing stanza is modeled as a zero-count nested epilogue rather than changing the work's primary prose form.

subject_statements.label: Murder of James I of Scotland and its aftermath
Punishment, torture, and execution of the conspirators
Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy

assertion.notes: The chronicle treats James's captivity, accession, governance, resistance to royal tyranny, murder, and the political claims surrounding the conspirators.

summaries.value: A contemporary or near-contemporary Middle English prose account of James I of Scotland's early life and English captivity, his return and government, the conspiracy against him, his murder at Blackfriars in Perth in February 1437, and the capture, torture, and execution of the conspirators. The closing counsel warns rulers against avarice and ties lordship to the people's love. John Shirley's Add MS 5467 colophon presents the work as his translation from a lost Latin narrative.

The repository XML encodes the 1818 tract's title page, a two-paragraph Advertisement on printed p. 3, and the historical text on printed pp. 5-29. The Advertisement and title page are edition-level framing; the cataloged work begins with Robert Steward and ends with Shirley's translator colophon. The text inherits the leaf-loss gap of Add MS 5467 even though complete readings survive in Add MS 38690 and Adv.MS.17.1.22.

The full XML has 2 DIV1, 37 P, 1 LG, 4 L, 27 PB, and 8 NOTE1 elements. Four P belong to the HEADER, two to the FRONT Advertisement, and 31 to the BODY chronicle. FRONT has two PB and BODY has 25. The second BODY milestone labeled p. 26 corresponds to printed p. 27 in the inspected 1818 scan; the header's Latin declaration and blanket Shirley authorship are not accepted as intrinsic work metadata.

extent.description: One prose chronicle represented by 31 BODY paragraphs, followed near its close by one four-line stanza and then the translator colophon. The sections value of 2 counts the XML's two DIV1 containers: the FRONT Advertisement and the BODY chronicle; it does not assert two intrinsic sections of the medieval work, and the separate TITLEPAGE is not a DIV1. The two zero-count FRONT paratext parts comprise that title page and the two-paragraph Advertisement. The encoding has 25 BODY page milestones for printed pp. 5-29, with p. 27 mislabeled as a second p. 26.

content_parts.label: 1818 title-page paratext
content_parts.summary: The standalone Glasgow tract title, French maxim, claim that the source manuscript was written in 1440, publisher, printer, place, and date.

locators.value: printed p. [1]; complete-volume PDF p. 275; Internet Archive BookReader n274

notes: The date claim MCCCCXL repeats Pinkerton's inference from a different Shirley translation in Add MS 5467 and is not the current BL date of that physical codex.

content_parts.label: Advertisement
content_parts.summary: An anonymous 1818 editorial notice repeats Pinkerton's provenance and dating rationale, says the tract first appeared in his History of Scotland, and disputes his proposal that the Latin relation was published in Scotland by authority.

locators.value: printed p. [3]; complete-volume PDF p. 277; Internet Archive BookReader n276

notes: This print-editorial prose is represented for navigational fidelity but contributes no work item, authorship, medieval date, or primary language facet.

content_parts.label: The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis
content_parts.summary: The Middle English account from Robert Steward and the accession background through James I's murder, the conspirators' punishment, closing counsel, and Shirley's translator colophon.

locators.value: TEXT/BODY/DIV1[@NODE='deathjas:2']; 31 P, excluding nested closing LG
printed pp. 5-29; complete-volume PDF pp. 279-303; Internet Archive BookReader n278-n302
ff. 72v-84v, with fragment f. 72* and a torn-leaf lacuna

notes: The prose metrics exclude the nested four-line counsel so that parent and child counts remain non-overlapping.
The XML visibly marks the Add MS 5467 leaf loss after the opening paragraph; the missing narrative is recoverable from the two complete witnesses but is not silently supplied here.

content_parts.label: Four-line closing counsel
content_parts.summary: A four-line stanza affirms that a ruler is truly a lord when loved by the people and closes with a commendation to God.
notes: Neilson prints a fuller prose-and-verse conclusion from Adv.MS.17.1.22. The four encoded lines belong to the Add MS 5467/Pinkerton/1818 transmission represented here.

related_works.title: Lost Latin narrative of the murder of James I of Scotland
notes: The Add MS 5467 colophon says Shirley translated out of Latin. No inspected source identifies a title, author, exemplar, or independently surviving Latin text, so this related work is not converted into a physical witness or assigned a Scottish publication history.

evidence.citation: XPath and structural inspection of CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/deathjas.xml.
evidence.locator: count(//DIV1), count(//P), count(//LG), count(//L), count(//PB), count(//NOTE1), FRONT and BODY scoped counts
evidence.summary: Reproducibly finds 2 DIV1, 37 P, 1 LG, 4 L, 27 PB, and 8 NOTE1. Four P are in HEADER, two are in the FRONT Advertisement, and 31 are in the BODY work. FRONT has two PB and BODY 25; direct scan comparison identifies the repeated p. 26 milestone as printed p. 27.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, The dethe of James Kynge of Scotis / translated by John Shirley, CME item deathjas.

evidence.citation: Miscellanea Scotica: A Collection of Tracts Relating to the History, Antiquities, Topography, and Literature of Scotland, vol. II (Glasgow: printed for and sold by John Wylie & Co.; R. Chapman, printer, 1818), complete University of Michigan / Google scan.

evidence.summary: Complete-volume evidence for the exact 1818 carrier, anonymous reset's title and Advertisement, John Wylie and Robert Chapman imprints, work boundary, page sequence, direct typographic source of the XML, repeated p. 26 encoding error, and reset relationship to Pinkerton.

evidence.citation: John Pinkerton, The History of Scotland from the Accession of the House of Stuart to That of Mary, with Appendixes of Original Papers, vol. I (London: C. Dilly, 1797), Appendix XIII, pp. 462-475.
evidence.locator: printed pp. 462-475; PDF pp. 479-492; Internet Archive BookReader n478-n491
evidence.summary: First-publication evidence for Pinkerton's direct Add MS 5467 transcription, explicit torn-leaf gap, Shirley translator/source note, manuscript provenance, inherited circa-1440 inference, editorial notes including the Perth objection, and the full text reset in 1818.

evidence.citation: British Library, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 5467, record 032-002029031.
evidence.locator: collection date and origin; physical description; item 6 at ff. 72v-84v; Digitised Content and access fields
evidence.summary: Current repository evidence for the mid-to-late-fifteenth-century English composite codex, after-1461 lower-bound clue, possible Rouland Brugge hand across ff. 23-224, chronicle locus, torn leaf and fragment f. 72*, Shirley translator colophon, complete Add MS 38690 witness, publication history, blank Digitised Content field, and physical-request route.

evidence.citation: British Library, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 38690, record 040-002058005.
evidence.locator: item 4 at ff. 9-15; physical description; Digitised Content and access fields
evidence.summary: Current repository evidence for the complete circa-1440 chronicle witness at ff. 9-15, its relation to Add MS 5467 and the latter's Shirley colophon, the 18-folio paper object formerly configured as a 22-foot-5-inch roll, blank Digitised Content field, and physical-request route.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Complaint made to Henry VI by the Duke of Gloucester, and A fulle lamentable cronicle, Adv.MS.17.1.22, resource 19596.
evidence.locator: record title, identifier, repository, and date exposed in the current browse result
evidence.summary: Current repository evidence identifying Adv.MS.17.1.22 as the combined Gloucester complaint and James I chronicle file and dating it to the sixteenth century. The item page returned HTTP 403 to the automated client, so no unexposed fields or digital-image claims are inferred.

evidence.citation: George Neilson, Missing Section of The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis, Recovered, Scottish Historical Review 2, no. 1 (October 1904): 97-99.
evidence.summary: Direct scholarly evidence that Pinkerton and Stevenson used Add MS 5467, that Adv.MS.17.1.22 was formerly Phillipps MS 27369 and contains a full text at ff. 13-25 in an unidentified seventeenth-century hand, and that its ff. 14-15 restore the missing passage and its conclusion supplies fuller readings. The seventeenth-century description conflicts with the current NLS sixteenth-century date.

evidence.citation: Lister M. Matheson, ed., Death and Dissent: Two Fifteenth-Century Chronicles (Woodbridge: Boydell Press, 1999), part I.
evidence.locator: publisher description; contents sample, part I, pp. 1-57
evidence.summary: Current publisher and contents evidence for the established title, Shirley translation from a lost Latin narrative, James I murder and execution subject, manuscript/source discussion, and modern text of Add MS 38690 collated with Add MS 5467.

evidence.citation: Margaret Connolly, The Dethe of the Kings of Scotis: A New Edition, Scottish Historical Review 71 (1992): 46-69.
evidence.locator: official publication metadata, title, journal, volume, pages, date, and keywords
evidence.summary: Institutional metadata confirms the 1992 peer-reviewed new edition, its established title variant, Middle English translation classification, Scottish-history subject, James I subject, and Shirley association. The article text was not publicly available through the inspected portal.

open_questions.question: Who composed the lost Latin narrative, where and when was it written, and what exact Latin exemplar did John Shirley translate?
next_steps: Review post-Matheson scholarship for proposed Latin analogues, political context, recipient, and translation date.
Keep the source as an unidentified related work rather than assigning an author, Scottish official publication, or physical witness without direct evidence.
Separate the date of Add MS 38690 from the translation date unless paleographic or textual evidence directly joins them.

open_questions.question: What is the exact relationship among Add MS 38690, Add MS 5467, and Adv.MS.17.1.22, and should the NLS copy be dated to the sixteenth or seventeenth century?
next_steps: Consult Matheson's full manuscript and stemmatic discussion and later paleographic work.
Ask the National Library of Scotland for the basis of its current sixteenth-century date and whether the script has been reassessed since Neilson's seventeenth-century description.
Compare the complete texts directly when full witness facsimiles or permitted reproductions become available.

open_questions.question: Should a future textual-encoding project supply or parallel the passage missing from the Add MS 5467-derived XML using Add MS 38690 or Adv.MS.17.1.22?
next_steps: Treat any supplied or parallel text as a separately authorized encoding change, not a metadata correction.
Choose an explicit edition and witness policy before combining readings from different witnesses.
Retain the present manifest's description of the checked-in defective version until such a project is completed.

notes: John Shirley is modeled as translator, not author; the author and physical exemplar of the lost Latin narrative remain unknown.

Add MS 5467 is the direct manuscript source of Pinkerton's 1797 publication, which is the textual source of the anonymous 1818 reset; the XML header's direct manuscript wording therefore compresses two print layers.
Add MS 38690 and Adv.MS.17.1.22 are complete collateral witnesses, not direct sources of the defective encoded text.
The 1818 Advertisement and title page remain visible as zero-count paratext so that the encoded boundary is documented without turning edition-level claims into medieval work facts.
The record is partial because the Latin source, translation circumstances, exact witness stemma, and NLS witness date remain unresolved; the encoded content structure itself is completely mapped.
